deductions.reason: Across all three conversations, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is withholding direct answers or walking the student through the process rather than giving them the answer—i.e., it never articulates that this approach supports active reasoning, retention, or skill development. It simply declines or redirects without naming the pedagogical rationale.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1 Turn 10, Transcript 2 Turn 10, Transcript 3 Turn 10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful progress (e.g., producing a workable draft actor list or a concrete hypothesis), the tutor moves immediately to the next step without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the approach that worked was effective.

In Transcript 2, the tutor cycles through the same 'pick one issue, classify by role, treat overlap as a finding' framework across multiple turns without meaningfully advancing the student's understanding or synthesizing progress. By Turn 10, the tutor explicitly acknowledges 'We've circled this idea a few times,' yet the pattern of repeating the same conceptual framing without consolidation persists throughout the conversation.

grade.overview: The tutor performs well on the core Socratic method criteria—it consistently refuses to do the assignment for students, avoids rewriting student text, and keeps key idea generation student-led across all three transcripts. Scaffolding is generally solid, building on student progress incrementally. The main weaknesses are: (1) the tutor never explains its pedagogical rationale for withholding answers, missing an opportunity to build student metacognition; (2) it does not prompt reflection after student breakthroughs; and (3) in Transcript 2 especially, the tutor cycles through the same conceptual framing repeatedly without consolidating or advancing, which the rubric penalizes under 2.1. The 2.1 deduction is applied once for the bundle based on the clearest evidence in Transcript 2, without double-penalizing across transcripts.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produces near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to generate ideas before confirming them. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on student progress (e.g., 'That's a workable start') and uses diagnostic questions before redirecting. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutor never articulates why it is guiding rather than answering (1.3.A.a), and across all three conversations it moves to the next task immediately after student improvement without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses 4 points: Transcript 2 shows clear pattern-level repetition—the tutor cycles the same 'pick one issue, classify by role, treat overlap as a finding' framing across six or more turns without synthesis, and even self-acknowledges the loop in Turn 10. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 receive full marks: the tutor stays on-task, keeps responses concise and structured, and maintains a supportive, non-grading tone throughout.
